Alright, your back time for your next lesson of advanced tonality. Ready? When we go to tonality number four, let's put the first three up. Number one was absolute certainty. Number two was then collapsing to utter sincerity. And then number three after that is called your reasonable man tone. Number four now is called your hypothetical money aside tonality. And this is a really awesome one. Here's the deal. Now in the back half of the sale, meaning after you ask for the order for the first time, you'll get hit with one of the common objections. We engage in a process called looping. And what looping allows you to do is take any one of those common objections and use it as an opportunity to build more certainty for each of the three tens and then seamlessly transition into a close and doing all of that without breaking report. Now the way we go about doing that is by applying that hypothetical money aside tonality to certain words that we say that allow us to do what's called deflect our prospects objection. In other words, at the end of the front half of the sale, you would ask for the order for the first time using that powerful three tonalities pattern, right? You know, you have certainty into sincerity into the reasonable man, right? And you say sound fair enough. Well what's the response to that? And the client will typically say, it sounds good. Let me think about it. Typical response. And that's what we call a compound response. Meaning they say, that sounds interesting. Let me think about it. Sounds interesting. Let me call you back. Sounds good. That's not a good time of year. That's a bad time of year. You get it? That's your compound response. Now when we engage in deflection, what we do is we don't answer that objection head on. We deflect it instead using the following language pattern. And I'll go through this in much more detail looping, but I want to use to teach you this tonality. It's hypothetical money aside tonality. So here's the pattern. You would say, I hear what you're saying, but let me ask you a question. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? One more time. I hear what you're saying, but let me ask you a question. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? Now notice implicit in the way I said that is that hypothetical money aside tonality. My very voice, my tone says, I understand you're not borrowing. I know all things being equal, hypothetically speaking. Let me ask you a question. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? So by doing that, I essentially remove the pressure and I'm saying, well, watch, here's a question. Let me ask you a question, Don. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? And he's like, whoa, maybe not. So what happens is if you apply an aggressive tonality, the prospect will almost down regulate their response. So you say it the right way. Here's the right way. Well, I hear what you're saying, but let me ask you a question. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? Your prospect will say, yeah, it sounds pretty good. It will give you an honest, forthright answer because there's no pressure. He feels comfortable enough that if he gives you that answer, you're not going to try to ram the product down his throat as opposed to the other way. Well, let me ask you a question. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? Well, does it make sense to you? Do you like the idea? Like, you're like, ah, right? You're going crazy. That's not going to work well. Or even John, does the idea make sense to you? No, that's sincerity. It's let me ask you a question. Are all things being equal? Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? By doing that, you essentially remove the pressure of having to buy right now from the situation, which then allows the prospect to feel comfortable enough to give you an honest answer. And then from there, we can go about now building more certainty. So that pattern of, you know, I hear what you're saying. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? That must be said with that hypothetical money aside tonality or else you're not going to get the correct response. You're going to get almost a down regulated response, meaning they'll purposely reduce the enthusiasm they put out to you because they're nervous that if they show you how they really feel, you'll use it as a pressure point to get them to buy. That's how powerful this is. In fact, I tell you a great story here. Back in the day after Stratton closed, when people took the scripts and the patterns that are spreading around the world, well, guess what? While they had my old scripts, they didn't have a key, so to speak, to have which tonality should be applied to which points. So that pattern of deflection, does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? If it's not said with the right tonality, it won't work. It'll actually blow up the sale. So what happened is that over the years, people started adding words on. They would say, well, Mr. Jones, all things beagle, hypothetically speaking, does it make sense to you? They had to add on these extra words. Now why would that be a problem? Well, guess what? When you add on those extra words, you're actually giving up control of the sale. What you're saying to the process is listen, Bill or Jim or John or Jill, I understand you're not buying right now. It's now an academic exercise. And just like that, you're done. You've given away control. So you don't want to have to say those words. So what do we do? We use the power of tonality and unspoken words. I hear what you're saying. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? And what the hearing is, money aside, hypothetically speaking, boom, there you have it. And that is your fourth tonality. And now let's go right to a quiz here. It's a very important one. We use this throughout the entire presentation, not just there. It's just one example. And then go for your exercise to make sure you have this locked in. And as soon as you do, I will see you in the next lesson.
